title: CSAR: Prof. Kay-Tee Khaw
text:
[Applause]
thank you very much for the kind
invitation to talk today I'm really
going to talk about population studies
there are lots and lots of articles
every day when they open the newspaper
about slowing aging animal studies etc
and three years ago or so there was a
lot of publicity from one of the first
articles based on UK biobank which is
nearly half a million people who
collected data and they had this
predictive questionnaire they simply
based on questions about whether you're
going to die in the next five years
a simple online test and you can go on
the website to do it now the questions
are pretty obvious I think if I asked
any of you what you would use to predict
future mortality its age obviously the
older you are yes and sex women do bit
better than men prevalent illness so if
you've got serious illnesses you're not
going to last as long so you know look
these are things that we've known about
for a long time but they were able to
devise this based on half a million
people in the UK I'm going to talk about
the small smaller cohort but when they
published all this stuff had me in the
headlines private I came out and I don't
know whether you've seen this about the
proof of tests that can predicted you
will die in the next five years and I
think you know given what's happening
more recent past I think this may still
hold even though this was 2015 so we're
going to go back to consider demographic
challenges which I'm sure you're all
very familiar with briefly but
justifying why it is so important then
I'm going to largely present results
from our cohort studies but bringing in
some other population studies and some
interim conclusions these are numbers
that are very familiar to all of us
because we hear about them all the time
but I just put some figures there to
show you the scale of this this is the
European Union countries which was still
in so far age distribution 2010 and 2060
to show you just in 50 years what a big
change
is going to be in the numbers of people
over 65 in over 80 years more than
doubling of some of these numbers so
it's a huge impact it's partly related
to changing birth rates and also longer
survival at later years so a huge
demographic change that is unprecedented
in human history but actually a great
success because the reason we're all
living longer is because we're not dying
in infancy from malnutrition infectious
disease diseases etc accidents and if we
look at the projected percent of the
population in hundred years more than 60
years in selected countries the profound
change if you have a society Japan that
in 1950 has 5 7 percent people aged more
than 60 years and by 2050 we'll have
nearly 1 in to 45 percent people aged
over 60 years and this is United Kingdom
which is about their United States isn't
quite such a big trend but all countries
are having this massive trend in
increasing numbers of older people and
the reason this is important is because
this is having a profound impact on
society all the structures that we have
which were designed for a very different
age pyramid 1950 was the year that I was
born for every person or every two
people age more than 65 years there were
24 people of working age and then also a
large proportion of children whereas for
2050 the projection is that for every
two people age more than 65 years there
will just be four people of working age
and one person under 15 years so it's a
massive shift in terms of the balance of
so-called working age population and
older people and that is going to have
such a profile which is having already a
huge impact on society and that's why
we're having to rethink all the
implications such as employment work
patterns taxation pensions huge fuss in
the universe about pensions now
education social structures
intergenerational relations because my
children your children are
not having the same benefits that the
baby-boom generation had in terms of
education health service etcetera health
care but the focus of this particular
talk is about health because health of
course is a major concern an aging
population is not a big issue if people
are generally well it's only an issue if
we are not well as we get older so
maintaining optimal health throughout
her life is really important and
understanding the environmental and
social determinants so the reason that
aging is an issue is because as we get
older and horizontal axis of that graph
as we get older the prevalence of
disability increases and this is
something we're very familiar with so by
the age over 85 these are different
degrees of disability ten being the most
severe nine to ten we can't look after
ourselves we can't feed ourselves but
even one is we can't walk as fast as we
used to we can't lift as heavy weights
as we used to and so the difficulty is
aging is associated with increased
likelihood of disability leading to
projections about cost of care and the
reason this is also important is that at
whatever age if we are age 70 65 now for
men and women we can expect to live
another 20 years based on current
projections age 75 we still have another
13 to 15 years to go on average age 85
another seven to eight years for men and
women and even at age 95 we still have a
couple of years ago so the longer we
live the more likely we are to live a
little bit more and at retirement age
the so-called standard retirement age we
might expect another two decades of life
and the reason this is again important
is because when we live these extra
years of life we want to make sure that
these are high quality lives free of as
far as possible of disability and what
is very marked is that using such a
crude indicator or
social deprivation which is just
postcodes based on your postcode you can
classify areas in the United Kingdom
according to deprivation that is
percentage of owner occupancy people on
benefits etc and if we look at the total
and healthy life expectancy in the least
deprived compared to the most deprived
10% of the population in the United
Kingdom you can see there that yes there
is a slight difference in life
expectancy for both men and women of
about five years depending on whether
you are living in the least deprived or
the most deprived areas I have a
colleague Michael Marmot who does
something on the central the tube line
central line in London which is that for
every stop along the central line you
lose one or two years of life expectancy
this is not as you go out into the more
deprived areas but what is really more
marked is that not just total life
expectancy but healthy life expectancy
if you live in the least deprived 10%
there for men and for women you might
expect of your 82 years or so of future
life expectancy to live 12 of those with
some sort of disability or some
compromised health unhealthy life
expectancy and for but the most deprived
double that almost 22 years lived with
not in good health for women again 14
years of that 80 something life
expectancy but nearly double those
numbers of years lived in poor health if
you live in a deprived area so one of
the most powerful determinants of life
expectancy but more importantly healthy
life expectancy is the area you live in
a really crude measure so this is not
magic the question is what is that that
is making such a big difference in the
expectancy of healthy life years ahead
so again the thing about my research
tool is an epidemiology aside study
populations human populations and what I
do essentially is ask people like all of
you what you
and then I pull her you up and home and
then we find out who did well and who
didn't according to what happened to
them and again this is all pretty common
sensical healthy life expectancy is
determined by a relatively limited
number of chronic conditions
cardiovascular disease cancer
neurological conditions such as dementia
blindness hearing loss respiratory
diseases musculoskeletal diseases such
as osteoporosis arthritis etc so chronic
disabling conditions contribute the most
to healthy life expectancy or unhealthy
life expectancy but virtually every
single condition that we know about and
I've just given you coronary heart
disease as one example because the rates
are really marked for women same for men
is that if we look at the rates with age
on the right hand the left of the
international league table and the
Eastern European countries have the
worst in terms of age adjusted rates of
coronary heart disease on the right is
the rate of coronary heart disease
according to age and so one of the
questions is horizontal axis age 45 to
74 as we get older we're more likely to
get heart disease but the question is
why is it that in Japan the rate of rise
is so much lower than that in the United
Kingdom or United States and of course
again Russia is by far the worst so by
the time they're 75 they have something
like six-fold differences in your
chances of getting coronary heart
disease so a Japanese person here in
this case women aged 75 has the same
chance of getting heart disease as
somebody 15 years younger in Russia so
there's a difference of 15 years in
terms of expectancy and there's
something about different environments
in which we live which have a profound
impact on how quickly or how badly we
age in terms of chronic disease anyway
so international variations time trends
migration studies and migration time
trends show that that just suggests that
the major determinants of healthy aging
are environmental not genetic and a
large body of evidence suggests
lifestyle factors
of course what are the obvious lifestyle
factors diet physical activity smoking
pollution etc may have a major impact so
Linda Partridge has done a lot of
biological reviews on the biological
measurements and aging based on animal
studies and this is just one example was
published in 2013 and she's just
published another really nice review in
Nature
about a week ago 2018 which I'll show
which is sort of emphasizing more or
less the same points but essentially we
can identify many of the cellular and
biological mechanisms that are
associated with aging when we study
aging animals or worms or mammals
genomic instability epigenetic
alterations etc so there are all these
mechanisms which have been identified
and their rafts of literature on all
these different Hormats of Aging
now the question is how do we translate
these biological mechanisms into
something that we can measure in free
living human populations and that may
have some relevance to how we act and
this is why Linda Partridge in her later
paper September 2018 this was the other
one was few years ago has developed this
further to discuss how these hallmarks
of aging here that we looked at earlier
might result in age-related expressions
such as loss of bone cartilage muscle
mass gain of abdominal fat mechanical
and structural changes lots of chronic
diseases and also discuss possible
interventions and challenges such as
genetics drugs that might influence some
of these processes diet and exercise so
that's the theory about what we can do
so these include dietary interventions
mimicking chronic dietary restriction
drugs that influence some of these axes
such as the hormone acts ease or growth
factor acts ease and some of these
pathways such as rapamycin activating
sirtuins
which are different mechanisms that have
implicated in aging and people are
exploring these resveratrol which is in
red wine that everyone talks about
though you do have to drink something
like four pints of red wine a day or
something to get anything like this
chosen my so that's but anyway those are
theoretical potential interventions the
difficulty is a lot of these are done in
laboratory animals or worms and how does
this apply to free living human
populations because rats for example
have very different metabolisms and this
has was a really ambitious study
everybody has been talking about caloric
restriction being the mechanism by which
we can prolong life so this is a really
really ambitious study the National
Institutes of aging study whereby they
took rhesus monkeys and for twenty years
they did a trial putting them on the
caloric restricted diet or usual diet
and their survival over twenty years and
they did this starting with young mice
and they also did this with old its not
by soe monkeys probably because monkeys
being more like humans here so this is
young onset caloric restriction monkeys
and all onset caloric restriction
monkeys the reds are female and the blue
are male and the dotted lines are
control and caloric restrictive caloric
restriction are dotted lines and so all
I can show you I mean what you can see
from bear is females survive longer than
males it's great here but those
absolutely no difference in caloric
restriction this is a 20-year study so a
lot of the things that seem promising
from the animal experiments when you try
them out in this 20-year trial on
primates caloric restriction made no
difference whatsoever in their 20-year
survival and this sort of caused some
consternation so we're back to the
drawing board because one of the
questions is we have all these theories
that we develop but how does this pan
out in real life human populations
because that's what we're really
interested in
so I'm really going to discuss some
results from our own study and the
European prospective investigation into
cancer and chronic disease was initially
started because there are huge
variations throughout Europe we saw some
of those earlier for heart disease but
also for cancer and it's
socioeconomically it's not so different
so the question was to try and identify
why different European countries have
such different experiences for heart
disease cancer and other chronic
diseases I'm really going to talk about
our own study which contributed to this
half million cohort and that's the epoch
Norfolk study which is the Norfolk based
component of the European prospective
investigation in which we recruited
25,000 men and women who were then aged
40 to 79 years and this started in 1993
so two weeks ago we had a 25th
anniversary celebration of this study so
all these men and women have now been
followed up for 25 yes and we had very
detailed measurements of exposures and
outcomes so they gave us a lot of blood
samples they've been filling in regular
questionnaires having measurements over
these 25 years and being followed up for
what happened to them so the model that
we have is very much based on the model
we saw earlier asking the question what
influences the rate of age-related
changes in health and disability we're
interested in preventing diseases and
disability we want to prevent strokes
fractures dementia or cancer and we go
back to say well why does one person get
cancer and not another
why doesn't in one person fracture one
why does one person get arthritis and
not another so we measured physiological
risk factors that might influence our
chances of developing these chronic
conditions and we all know about
cholesterol blood pressure etc but we
also measure things like lung function
why does our lung function decline with
age why does our blood pressure go up
with age why does our control of lipids
decline with age why does I immune
function get worse as we get older why
do our blood vessels clog up as we get
older our clotting changes and to go
back from that to measure these means a
lot
your risk factors in this population but
to ask what is it that influences how
everyone in this population ages what
are the exposures the dietary factors
the lifestyle psychosocial physical
activity etc and genetic now you will
all have heard of Elizabeth Blackburn
who won a Nobel Prize for telomeres and
she has got a thing for measurement of
telomeres and predicting biological age
four hundred pound test that tells you
how long you'll age we were able to do
whole genome studies in the entire
population and we have now looked at all
the genes for heart disease obesity
physical activity it's just amazing that
2007 this was one of the first whole
genome studies that was just 11 years
ago and since then we've had hundreds
and hundreds of papers looking at genome
Association studies for all sorts of
trays like heart disease cancer dementia
etc but this was one of the earliest
saying a common variant in the FTO gene
is associated with body mass index and
predisposes to childhood obesity and
adult obesity so genes and obesity the
point I want to make here is that we
measure these genes in the cohort and
what we can show is we can do 27 genes
that we measured and you can score a
genetic profile depending on how many of
these susceptibility genes you have the
blue that the dark shaded parts are the
people who had high genetic
susceptibility very high genetic score
for obesity and the white are the people
who had a lower gene score lower genetic
susceptibility so the first thing I want
to point out is that people who had the
high genetic score were on average
fatter than the people that the low
genetic score on both sides you can see
that higher mean body mass index but the
second point I want to make from here is
that when we divided people into people
who were generally inactive or active
the people who were active on the right
hand side had sorry had lower
average BMI and the people who were
inactive so the people active with the
high genetics school had lower body mass
index compared to people who were
inactive and have the low genetic score
so the point of this is that your
lifestyle here physical activity totally
overwhelmed the impact of your genetic
susceptibility to obesity so physical
activity was much more powerful in
predicting the magnitude of body mass
index
if you're active compared to inactive
compared to the genetics core so it's
just one example but we've done that for
several other trays one of the questions
that they asked in UK biobank was how do
you feel about that health and one of
the criticisms will always have in
epidemiologic studies is that we use
soft data we just ask people questions
we we have biological measures but we
ask people questions and what is
remarkable is that people assess their
own health really well at the baseline
we just asked individuals how do you
assess your own health do you feel good
about your health is an excellent good
moderate or poor and then we followed
them up for the next 12 years and this
is the relative risk of mortality
according to excellent good moderate of
work so if people self-reported poor
health at baseline and this is excluding
people who are did other illnesses and
age adjusted etc you can see that both
for men and women people who
self-reported not feeling good about the
health poor health and about five times
the subsequent risk of mortality over
the next 12 years even though they had
no apparent other illnesses compared to
people who had self-reported excellent
health and as a gradient across whether
it was accident good moderate of work so
the point here is that self-reported
subjective health is very strongly
related to objective film at least as
measured as subsequent mortality strong
relationship and that Association was
much much stronger than any of the blood
tests that have been suggested that
opposed to predict many of these aging
things such as you know aging test
scientists developed blood tests from
RNA none of these have been many of them
have not been tested long-term but the
ones have been tested I'm not nearly as
powerful as self-reported earth health
but we also wanted to measure other
things so we measured simple low-cost
physiological measures that are very
good indicators of aging and these are
things like lung function you just blow
into a spirograph measure your pulse
rate a low pulse rate is a very good
indicator of good health good lung
function good bone health low blood
glucose levels controlling blood glucose
levels and low levels of inflammation
again measured by a very simple blood
test c-reactive protein or your total
white count so these really simple tests
that anyone can do in a GP surgery are
as powerful predictors of subsequent
aging and mortality as any of these new
fangled very sophisticated developed
tests just to give you some examples
good lung function predicts good
self-reported physical function we we
both asked about self-reported physical
function about things that people could
do but we also measured this objectively
in terms of grip strength etc so we just
divided people into fifths of lung
function and the people who had the best
lung function here right in the top
fifth top 20% compared to bottom 21st
was graded across the whole relationship
we're much more likely to report
themselves in as having good physical
function so good lung function predicts
good physical quality of life function
good lung function also predicts lower
hip fracture risk so having a good lung
function followed up these people these
participants for 10 years in this case
the people who had the best lung
function here the top 25% compared to
bottom 25% and about half the subsequent
risk of hip fracture for both men and
women blue men and red women compared to
people who are in the bottom 25% so
there's across the whole normal
distribution we're not talking about
people who had lung
disease or or people really ill these
are healthy normal people and lung
function was a really powerful predictor
of subsequent fracture risk we measure
glycated hemoglobin which is an
indicator of control of blood glucose
and for both men and women over the next
eight years this was the study but the
higher your glycated hemoglobin which is
a marker of your average blood glucose
the subsequent in the past three months
the higher your subsequent mortality now
this is the level at which people have
diagnosed as diabetes but even below the
level at which you might be construed as
having clinical diabetes across the
whole gradient of blood sugar the higher
your average blood sugar levels the
higher your subsequent ill health
mortality so controlling blood sugar
levels seem to be important and there's
another example of c-reactive protein
and inflammatory marker again I'm just
sizing for some of these markers very
similar that the higher your c-reactive
protein the higher your mortality over
the next again eight years or so and
another one this is a really simple test
heel ultrasound which you can have in
any GP surgery you just stick your heel
in it measures density of your heel bone
and that is a really powerful predictor
of subsequent risk of fracture so the
better your heel ultrasound the lower
your fracture risk there's about five
whole difference in fracture is between
people who are bottom 10% and people are
top 30% for heel ultrasound so really
simple physiological tests that any GP
can do are really powerful predictors of
subsequent disabling conditions chronic
disabling conditions but of course what
we're really interested in is can we
influence this what are the behavioral
factors that may relate to health and to
physiological markers so we measured
physical activity diet smoking stress
etc and our participants were great they
gave us blood samples to measure a lot
of the biomarkers of diet and they gave
us a lot of information about physical
activity more recently they've been
wearing accelerometers
but also the question is how big is the
magnitude effect of a feasible change so
for example smoking everybody knows
smoking is bad but we were able to
actually measure the magnitude of
effects in terms of lung function we've
already seen earlier that lung function
is a really good predictor of both
mortality even after adjusting for
smoking and of functional health quality
of life this is the mean lung function
yeah in non-smokers and current smokers
and on average throughout the whole
lifespan I mean the whole age band sorry
non-smokers have better average lung
function than current smokers both
groups decline with age but on average
non-smokers have much better average
lung function and this is the prevalence
of those people with poor lung function
according to smoking status so
essentially what this is really saying
is that if we look at this line compared
to this line
smoking is equivalent to about 10
biological years of life of age in terms
of lung function so if you are smoker
your lungs are 10 years older in terms
of lung function compared to a nonsmoker
in terms of both function and in terms
of predicting of the future physical
activity we were interested in not in
marathon running but in the daily
activity of that people do in their
everyday life this this is not something
that it's not about going to the gym
it's not a it's whether you cycle
regularly or not whether you climb
stairs how sedentary are you in terms of
how many hours do you sit watching
television and here lung function is
better this is average lung function in
men and women who climb more so the more
flexible says they report it climbing a
day the better the aboriginal function
they get this is again all age adjusted
so if you climb more flights of stairs a
day your lung functions better so daily
regular small amounts of physical
activity the more sedentary
you are in terms of television viewing
hours reported a day the worst your lung
function and this is not because sick
people don't climb stairs and sick
people sit and watch television the
whole day because this is across the
whole normal distribution these are
again in healthy people apparently
according to what they did in their
everyday life but it's not just
functional physiological measures we
also looked at how self-reported
physical activity predicted subsequent
mortality and this is a 10-year
follow-up and our measures of physical
activity were really moderate you are
classified as inactive if you do nothing
at all if your total couch potato if you
are moderately inactive all you have to
do is have a standing job like a
hairdresser or what all cycle less than
an hour a day so you know
listen hour a day and moderately active
was more than hour a day of cycling or
some other activity like gardening so
this free living population were not
incredibly active this was just very
very moderate differences in physical
activity just cycling a bit half now
every day or just having a standing
occupation was associated already here
between the first two with a 20% lower
mortality and then the more active if
you had a manual labor occupation then
that was more physical activity and also
the relative risk for good physical
function and again this is like quality
of life in terms of physical performance
things that people care about how fast
can they walk and they do things by
physical activity and smoking and here
you can see that people who are never
smokers have report much better quality
of a good physical function compared to
current smokers and people active report
much better physical activity compared
to people who are inactive and if we
look at the age gradient current smoking
is equivalent to being 7 years older or
nonsmoker is 7 years younger than a
current smoker in terms of physical
performance and physical inactivity
is equivalent to being 10 years older in
terms of physical performance so if
you're physically active you are 10
years younger in terms of your
functional performance compared to
somebody who isn't at the in
chronological age chair stands there's
another test we did and again this is a
very check you get up and down you count
how many steps you have meter says you
can do in 10 seconds or or and again
this was chair stands performance in
2007 according to their self-reported
physical activity 10 years ago so it was
what they were doing 10 years ago did
that predict how well they did chair
sounds and we know chair stands are very
very good predictor of physical function
and quality of life active compared to
inactive was equivalent to 7 years
younger in age in terms of performance
so this this was just some attempt to
put numbers on this and this is not just
our study I'm just doing a review no
other studies have put together a
meta-analysis or reviews suggesting
indeed that exercise different sorts of
exercise may have a profound impact on
physical function and daily living
activities in frail older adults that
people who do exercise or any sort of
physical activity do much better in
terms of quality of life people are
really interested in cognitive functions
so our participants in eight different
cognitive tests which measure different
things but the one I'm showing you is
the result for power which is paired
associates learning which is developed
from baboons so you don't need education
to do it it's matching shapes and
remembering shapes and we count the
number of errors and on the left you can
see that as we get older we make more
errors yeah age we get more errors but
if we have no qualifications educational
qualifications compared to tertiary
qualifications there's a big average
difference so people who have no
qualifications again both educated and
uneducated people make more mistake
as we get older but there is the
prevalence of the number of errors are
very different according to whether they
had no qualifications or tertiary
qualifications and the prevalence of
people scoring poorly is much lower the
percentage scoring well is much higher
in people who are tertiary compared to
no qualifications so compared to no
complications finishing school was
equivalent to 9 years younger in terms
of cognitive performance and tertiary
equivalent to certain equipment to 14
years younger in cognitive performance
now the question is why should educate
which was completed 15 or 60 years ago
have such a profound impact on cognitive
function now is it because early
education puts increases your brain
resilience and and potential or is it
because early education determines what
you do lifelong throughout life or you
know what is it about really early
experiences because a lot of these were
completed education up to age 20 and
this is performance at age 70 60 but but
it's very interesting so if maybe if we
want to prevent or delay dementia we
might really consider starting very
young anyway and and a lot of what I'm
talking about it's not about prevention
or it's about postponement it's such as
heart disease we don't want to get a
heart attack at forty five or even fifty
five but if we have a heart attack at
ninety five a lot of us will be dead
before then so it doesn't matter in any
way or or you know we will die of a
heart attack that's not so bad and
similarly with dementia we do not want
to get cognitive impairment at forty or
fifty or sixty but if we can postpone
the onset of dementia by five years or
six years so that most people will be
have died of other reasons other causes
before they get to the point of having
dementia so a lot of aging is
postponement of onset of chronic
disability and the estimates are for
example if we can delay the on
set of dementia by just two years we can
reduce the prevalence of dementia by 20%
if we can delay the onset of dementia by
five years we can reduce the prevalence
of dementia in the general population by
50% so just simple postponement of some
of these chronic conditions could have a
profound impact on the number of people
who are suffering with that condition
and again this is not just our study but
there many other studies that show that
high mental activity is associated with
lower risk of dementia whether it's
education occupational activity leisure
time activity etc but you can see the
summary is that it's protective for
dementia and these are many many other
studies that have been done in the
review and it's it's interesting again
referring to early education and having
more connections in your brain that the
white matter volume for different brain
regions in bilinguals and monolinguals
age seventy years it is is very
different that if you are bilingual you
have far more connections in your brain
and and also different volume and again
is it about making more connections the
more hobbies you have the more things
that you learn the more resilience your
brain has to deterioration and this is
just to VM ffice eyes the projected cost
by year that if we have an intervention
that delays onset by two years and by
five years that we have substantial
impact on the population in terms of
savings in terms of dementia support and
the good news is that actually from the
surveys both done in the United States
and in United Kingdom that the
prevalence of dementia in England and in
America over time in two time periods
seems to be declining so actually even
though numbers of people with dementia
are increasing because they're more old
people who are more at risk the actual
age-related rates of dementia are
actually declining so people are
actually better now or having less risk
of dementia now at a particular age than
they were 10 years ago so something good
is happening but they're more old people
so that's why we're seeing a rise in
dementia cases
so I've talked a bit about physical
activity diet we measured blood vitamin
C not because we think vitamin C is good
but because it's a very good biological
marker of fruit and vegetables because
